segments.s719-g89: به + noun phrase here marks the manner or circumstance ('with' / 'in the state of'). زاری و سستی is a coordinated noun phrase meaning 'lamentation and weakness/feebleness'. زبان برگشاد is a verb phrase where زبان (lit. 'tongue') is the thing acted on and برگشاد (past form) is the verb 'opened'—together: 'he opened his tongue (i.e. began to speak) in lamentation and weakness.'
segments.s719-g90: چنین گفت is a formula meaning 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so', گفت = past tense of گفتن 'he said'). کای is the archaic vocative 'O'. خواهر پاک is the addressee phrase 'pure/holy sister' (خواهر = 'sister', پاک = 'pure'). وراد here appears as a proper name or epithet following the vocative; syntactically it completes the address: 'O pure sister, Varad.'
segments.s719-g91: ز + noun (ز پند تو) means 'from' or 'as for' your advice ('پند تو' = 'your counsel/advice'). کمی here qualifies the outcome ('a little' / 'little effect'). نبد is an archaic negative past copula meaning 'was not' or 'did not become'. هیچ‌چیز is the negated substantive 'nothing.' The whole clause: 'From your advice there was little — nothing.'
segments.s719-g92: ولیکن = 'but / however.' مرا (here split in the data as م + را) is the first-person object 'me' (را is the object marker). خود adds emphasis/reflexivity 'myself / to me personally.' پر آمد is a compound verbal phrase: پر (full (of)) + آمد (came) meaning 'came full' or 'was filled.' قفیز is a rare/obscure noun here (left untranslated in the glossary); syntactically it is the thing that 'came' to him. Overall: 'But to me myself a qafiz came full.'
segments.s719-g93: همی is an emphatic or durative particle (here reinforcing the statement). پند 'advice' is the subject; بر من ('upon me' / 'to me') marks the affected party. نبد = 'was not' (arch. past negative). کارگر here means 'effective' (not 'worker') so the clause states that the advice 'was not effective for me.'
segments.s719-g94: ز هر گونه = 'from every kind / of every sort' (ز = 'from', هر گونه = 'every kind/manner'). چون introduces a simile 'like/as.' دیو بد راه is a noun phrase 'an evil/ill-path demon' or figuratively 'a fiend of the bad way' (دیو = demon/fiend, بد راه = 'of bad path' = 'ill-doing' or 'evil way'). بر at the end is a case particle meaning 'upon/toward' that links back to بر من in the previous clause (idiomatic positioning).
segments.s719-g95: نبد again is the archaic negative 'was not.' خسروی here means 'a royal/sovereign person' (i.e. 'sovereign/king'). برتر 'higher/superior' is a comparative. از جمشید = 'than Jamshid' (از + name marks the standard of comparison). Together: 'There was no sovereign superior to Jamshid.'
segments.s719-g96: کزو = 'from which / from whom' (referring back to Jamshid). بود is the past copula 'was.' گیتی 'the world' is the subject of the following state. به بیم وامید is a prepositional phrase 'in fear and hope' (به = 'in / with', بیم = 'fear', وامید = 'and hope' or 'with hope'). Altogether: 'From whom the world was in fear and hope.'
segments.s719-g65: الا‌ای is an archaic poetic vocative: الا ("behold/lo") + ای (vocative "O"). برآورده is a past-participial adjective here (literally "raised/produced/ brought up") modifying the following noun phrase. کوه بلند is the head noun + adjective (noun then adjective is common in poetry): "a lofty/tall mountain." The whole segment is a vocative interjection addressing the mountain.
segments.s719-g66: ز is the poetic preposition "from/of." دریای is the genitive construct (دریا + -ی) "sea of." خوشاب is a compound/name "sweet/pleasant water (sea)." بیخت here is an epithet attached to the sea‑phrase (poetic/archaic); in the lemma list it carries negative senses, but grammatically it functions as an adjective modifying دریای خوشاب. که کند begins a relative clause: که ("which/that") + کند (3rd‑singular present/epic present "does/causes").
segments.s719-g67: که starts the relative clause already begun in the previous line ("which/that"). کند is the 3rd‑person singular present/epic present "makes/causes." این‌چنین is a demonstrative/adverb "thus/so/such." سبز is an adjective "green/verdant." سرو is the noun "cypress" and سهی is a poetic epithet attached to سرو (here functioning as an adjectival epithet, not a literal color). Word order is typical poetic inversion but maps directly to English "that makes such a green, dark cypress."
segments.s719-g68: که again links the subordinate clause "that…" to the following verb. افگند is the past tense "threw/cast (down)." خوار is an adjective here meaning "lowly/despised/overthrown." این is the demonstrative "this." کلاه مهی is a compound: کلاه "cap/crown" modified by مهی ("moon‑like," i.e. resembling the moon). The clause means literally "that cast down/despised this moon‑like cap."
segments.s719-g69: که again begins a clause. آگند is a predicate adjective/participial form meaning "filled/replete" (here "was filled"). ناگاه is an adverb "suddenly/unexpectedly." دریا is the subject "the sea." به خاک is the prepositional phrase "with/to earth," where به takes the meaning "into/with" in poetic usage. The phrase describes the sea becoming filled/charged with earth unexpectedly.
segments.s719-g70: که ("which/that") introduces the next result clause. افگند is the past tense verb "threw/cast." کوه روان is a compound meaning "the mountain in motion/flowing mountain" (روان here = "moving/flowing"). در is the preposition "into/in" and مغاک is a poetic noun "abyss/gulf." The clause describes the consequent image: the moving mountain hurled into the abyss.
segments.s719-g71: غریبیم is 1st‑person plural present/predicative "we are strange/strangers/alien." The following و (and) links adjectives; تنها is "alone/solitary." و then links to the prepositional phrase بی دوستدار: بی ("without") + دوستدار (here "supporter/one who favors/attaches to you"). Grammatically this is a coordinate description of the speaker(s): "we are strangers and alone and without a supporter."
segments.s719-g72: بشهر is the locative compound (ب + شهر) meaning "in the city/among the city." کسان is "people/folk." در before the verb bماندیم is an archaic placement of the locative/particle; بماندیم is the 1st‑person plural past (perfective) "we remained/continued to be." خوار is the predicate adjective "low/despised." Together: "among the city's people we remained despised."
segments.s719-g33: Word order: subject (قلون) + past verb رفت + instrumental phrase با کارد + locative در آستی. با here marks the instrument ('with/by means of a knife'), and در + آستی gives a locative ('in the sleeve') — آستی is an archaic/poetic form for 'sleeve' (آستین). The verb رفت is simple past 'went/left' describing the motion or departure of the named thing/person.
segments.s719-g34: پدیدار شد is a compound verbal idea: پدیدار (manifest/visible) + شد (become) — together 'became manifest/appeared'. The following nouns کژی و کاستی are the things that 'appeared' (subject/complement). Poetic inversion places the verb before these nouns.
segments.s719-g35: همی‌رفت is an imperfective/past progressive ('was going'). تا introduces purpose ('in order to / so that'), so تا + verb expresses intended action: راز گوید بگوش = 'to tell (the) secret in the ear' (گوید here functions as the verb 'to say/tell' and بگوش is an adverbial phrase 'into the ear').
segments.s719-g36: بزد دشنه is a simple past clause 'he struck (بزد) [with] a dagger (دشنه)' — the instrument is explicit. وز here functions as an archaic/prepositional 'from' (literally 'from the house'); برشد خروش = 'a cry rose' (برشد = 'rose/was raised' + خروش = 'a cry/uproar'). Note the poetic order: the source phrase (وز خانه) precedes the intransitive result 'برشد خروش'.
segments.s719-g37: چو is a subordinating particle 'when'; it introduces the temporal clause چو بهرام گفت آه = 'when Bahram uttered “Ah”'. گفت here is the reporting verb, and آه is an interjection/sigh uttered by Bahram. مردم ز راه is a noun phrase 'the people from the road' that will be the subject of the following main clause.
segments.s719-g38: برفتند is past plural 'they departed/they went away'. پویان here is adverbial/qualifying ('swiftly, moving'), modifying how they left; به نزدیک شاه is a prepositional phrase 'toward/near the king' (به = to/toward, نزدیک = near, شاه = king). The clause thus describes the people's movement toward the king.
segments.s719-g39: چنین گفت is a formulaic 'he said thus/so'. کاین را بگیرید uses کاین را as the accusative object ('this/that one' + object marker را) placed before the verb; بگیرید here is a hortative/imperative plural 'take/seize (him/it)'. زود is an adverb 'quickly/at once' modifying the command.
segments.s719-g40: بپرسید زو is an imperative 'ask/question him' (بپرسید) where زو = ز + او 'from him/of him'. تا که introduces a purpose/result clause 'until/so that', leading to راهش نمود = 'he shows the way' (راهش = 'his/the way'; نمود is past 'showed/manifested'). Together: 'question him until he shows the way.'
segments.s719-g153: که introduces a subordinate reported clause (‘that’). The clause has subject من, the prepositional phrase با تو (‘toward/with you’), the adverb هرگز (‘never’) before the past verb نکردم (1st‑person past negative of کردن), and the object بدی (‘wrong/harm’) following the verb — a common Classical Persian order where adverbials and objects can appear before or after the verb.
segments.s719-g154: همی is an archaic/epic particle that gives an ongoing or emphatic flavor to the verb (common in Ferdowsi). راستی and بخردی are direct objects of جستم (past ‘I sought’), joined by و. The clause therefore reads ‘I sought truth and wisdom’; jستم is the simple past 1st‑person of جستن in the sense ‘to seek’.
segments.s719-g155: بسی here is a classical quantifier (‘many/much’). پندها is the plural object ‘advices/admonitions’, and خواند is the past verb ‘he recited/said’. بر خواهرش is the indirect object (‘to/upon his sister’) marked by the preposition بر plus the possessive suffix ش.
segments.s719-g156: ببر is the noun ‘the tiger’ as subject. در is the locative preposition ‘in/within’ (here indicating the tiger’s mouth/jaws/in its grip in poetic compression), گرفت is the past ‘seized/took’, and آن گرامی سرش = ‘that honorable/noble head of hers/his’ (آن + adjective + head + possessive). Word order places the subject before verb and the possessed noun phrase after the verb.
segments.s719-g157: دهن ‘mouth’ is the thing placed; بر is the preposition ‘on/against’. بنا گوش is an archaic/compound expression meaning the rim/edge of the ear (here translated ‘the ear’s edge’); نهاد is the past of نهادن ‘to place/put’ (3rd‑person singular past). The verb follows the object phrase, as is common in poetic diction.
segments.s719-g158: دو modifies چشمش (‘her two eyes’). پر از خون شد is a stative phrase using شدن ‘to become’ (شد) meaning ‘became full of blood’. و links the clause to جان بداد, where جان دادن (‘to give one’s life’) is an idiom for dying — بداد here functions as the past ‘gave’ in that idiom, so ‘she gave up her life’. This is an idiomatic past narrative construction.
segments.s719-g159: برو here functions as a narrative motion word (‘then departed/they went’) attached to the clause; هر کسی is the distributive subject ‘each person/everyone’, زار modifies the verb as an adverbial ‘lamenting/wretchedly’, and بگریستند is the past plural ‘they wept/wailed’. Overall: ‘each one went off and wept bitterly.’
segments.s719-g160: به درد دل is an idiomatic prepositional phrase ‘with a heart full of pain’ (به = with/into, درد = sorrow, دل = heart). اندر is an older preposition ‘in/within’, and همی‌زیستند is the plural past imperfective with the epic particle همی, meaning ‘they lived (together)/they coexisted’ — here ‘they continued living together in sorrow.’
segments.s719-g169: چنین است — a fixed predicate meaning “such is” or “so it is.” کار + سرای + سپنج is a noun phrase: کار (“work/affair”) qualified by سرای (from the verb سرودن/سراییدن, “to compose/sing”), so سرای here functions like “the composing/singing of.” سپنج is the name or label being qualified (likely a proper name or epithet). The whole segment means “Such is the work of Sepanj (the singer/poet).”
segments.s719-g170: چودانی is a contraction of چو دانی (“as you know”); که introduces the subordinate clause with a causal sense (“since/because”). ایدر is an archaic/poetic adverb meaning “here.” نمانی is 2nd-person-singular present (negative form with initial ن-) from ماندن “to remain,” giving “you do not remain” (i.e. you will not stay). مرنج is a negative imperative—“do not be upset/angry.” Together: “As you know, since you do not remain here, do not be upset.”
segments.s719-g1: This clause has an agent (قلون) followed by a past transitive verb (بستد) and its direct object phrase (آن مهر و تازان). In poetic word order the verb precedes some complements; بستد is a simple past 'tied/fastened' or 'put on.' آن = 'that' points to a particular thing(s). مهر + و + تازان coordinate two nouns ('mehr' and 'tāzān'); چو غرو begins a temporal subordinate phrase 'when dusk/at twilight.'
segments.s719-g2: The verb بیامد is a past tense 'came' (simple past). ز شهر کشان is an ablative + participial phrase: ز شهر = 'from the city' and کشان (a present/adverbial participle) means 'dragging/leading' and modifies the motion verb. تا به مرو marks extent/destination 'as far as / to Merv.'
segments.s719-g3: همی‌بود is an iterative/imperfective past meaning 'it was/continued to be.' تا introduces the temporal limit 'until,' followed by روز بهرام 'the day of Bahram.' شد here is the simple past of شدن 'to happen/arrive' and yields the sense 'the day of Bahram arrived.'
segments.s719-g4: که introduces a subordinate clause ('when/that'). بهرام را uses the object/topic marker را with the name 'Bahram' to mark the affected person. آن is the demonstrative 'that.' نه is the negative particle 'not' modifying the following name, and پدرام شد is a copular construction 'became Pedram' (شد = 'became'). The whole clause therefore says that, in that circumstance, 'that did not become Pedram for Bahram.' (This line uses compressed, archaic/poetic syntax.)
segments.s719-g5: به خانه درون is a locative phrase with به + noun + درون 'into/within the house' (poetic order). بود is the past-tense copula 'was.' با یک رهی is an accompanying phrase 'with a wayfarer/a traveller' where یک is the indefinite numeral and رهی is an archaic word for 'wayfarer.'
segments.s719-g6: نهاده is a past participle/verb meaning 'placed/put.' برش in this context is poetic contraction of بر + -ش (i.e. 'upon/for him') rather than the unrelated noun 'slice'; here it functions as a dative/locative 'before him/for him.' نار و سیب و بهی are coordinated items placed before him: نار 'fire,' سیب 'apple,' بهی 'quince' (بهی used as a fruit).
segments.s719-g7: Agent قلون (proper name) + past verb رفت 'went.' تنها is an adverb/adjective 'alone/only.' بدرگاه اوی is a prepositional/locative phrase meaning 'into his presence/into the royal court' where بدرگاه = 'in the presence (of a sovereign)' and اوی is the archaic possessive 'his.'
segments.s719-g8: به دربان is a prepositional phrase 'to the gatekeeper.' چنین گفت is the formulaic 'said thus/so' (چنین = 'thus/so'; گفت here functions as 'said' — the lexical list provided shows conversational senses but context is verbal 'he said'). کای is the vocative particle 'O,' and نامجوی is an epithet/name meaning 'name‑seeker' (one who seeks renown) addressed to the gatekeeper.
segments.s719-g73: همی‌گفتم is an archaic/prosaic imperfective past meaning “I was saying / I kept saying.” ای + خسرو is a vocative phrase (“O Khosrow”); انجمن follows as an epithet/apposition (literally “of the council/assembly”), so the whole chunk is a vocative addressed to the king.
segments.s719-g74: که introduces the following admonition (“that/that is, …”); شاخ here means “branch” (metaphorical); وفا is an abstract noun “fidelity/loyalty.” را is the definite/object marker attaching to شاخ وفا; تو is the emphatic/poetic second‑person subject “you”; از + بن means “from the root/base”; مکن is the negative imperative “do not (do).” The clause therefore gives a direct negative command to the addressee.
segments.s719-g75: که here continues/explains the previous thought (“for / that”); از تخم ساسان literally “from the seed/of the stock of Sasan” (prepositional phrase giving origin). اگر is the conditional particle “if,” and دختری is the indefinite noun “a daughter / a girl” (subject of the conditional). Note the slightly inverted, classical word order where the conditional particle follows the origin phrase.
segments.s719-g76: بماند is the present/past‑tense verb “remains / be left” used in a conditional sense with اگر (“if … remains”). به سر برنهد is a verbal phrase: به سر = “on (her) head,” برنهد (classical form) = “she will place / put upon / lift onto” (future/irrealis sense). افسری is the object “a diadem/helmet.” Together: “if a daughter remains, she will place a diadem on her head.”
segments.s719-g77: همه شهر ایرانش literally “all the cities of his/that Iran” (ایرانش = Iran + third‑person possessive/attributive). فرمان برند is a classical idiom: فرمان (command/order) + برند (they take/carry) → “they will take/obey the command (from that line).” Word order places the wide subject phrase up front, then the verb.
segments.s719-g78: ازان = “from that / of that”; تخمه here means “offspring / stock” (descendants). هرگز به دل نگذرند is a negative idiom: هرگز = “never”; به دل گذشتن / نگذرند = “to pass into the heart / to be accepted emotionally” — so the clause says they will never let that stock enter/settle in their hearts.
segments.s719-g79: سپهدار is the title “spahbed / commander.” نشنید is the simple past negative “did not hear / did not heed.” پند مرا is the object phrase “my counsel / my admonition” (مرا = me/my as object); here the verb negates that the commander heard the speaker’s advice.
segments.s719-g80: سخن گفتن is a verbal noun + infinitive “to speak / speaking.” سودمند is the adjective “useful / beneficial.” مرا here marks the experiencer/beneficiary (“for me”), so the phrase states that speaking was useful to the speaker — i.e., it was worthwhile for him to speak even though the commander did not heed him.
segments.s719-g41: This clause sets up the actors and scene: برفتند is a past 3rd-person plural verb ('they advanced/rushed'); هرکس که is a relative phrase 'everyone who/each one who' modifying the following description; بد در سرای is a prepositional phrase 'bad in the house' (i.e. those who had been wicked in the house). Word order is typical older Persian: verb first for dramatic effect, followed by the quantified relative phrase and the location.
segments.s719-g42: This clause names the victim and the violent action. مران پیر is appositional: a name plus the epithet 'pîr' ('the old/elder, i.e. the old man Maran'). سر را is the direct object marked by the object marker را, and پای is the second object (no repeat را is required in Persian). شکستند is past 3rd-person plural 'they broke/beat (into pieces)'. The overall structure: [agent (from previous segment)] + مران پیر (patient) + سر را شکستند پای (they broke his head and feet).
segments.s719-g43: همه کهتران is the plural subject 'all the younger ones/younger men'. زو is the prepositional/compositive element 'زِ + او' (here 'from him' / 'at him' or 'toward him') and functions together with بر آشوفتند: بر آشوفتند is a past 3rd-person plural intransitive/causative form meaning 'they rushed upon' or 'they fell upon' (آشوفتن = to be thrown into disorder/ to rush). The clause therefore means 'all the younger men rushed upon him.'
segments.s719-g44: This clause gives the manner of attack. به marks the instrument/means ('with' or 'by'); سیلی و مشتش is a coordination of instruments 'slap(s) and his/ their fist(s)' (مشت+ـش is the pronominal/clitic suffix indicating 'his/their fist'); بسی is an adverb indicating quantity/intensity ('many/much' or 'plenty'); کوفتند is past 3rd-person plural 'they struck/beat'. Together: 'they struck with slaps and many blows of the fist.'
segments.s719-g45: همی‌خورد is a classical continuous past form meaning 'he kept receiving / was continually subjected to' (here used with سیلی to mean 'kept getting slapped'). سیلی is the object 'slap(s)'. نگشاد is a negative perfect/past form (ن + گشاد) meaning 'did not open'; here it governs لب 'his lips' — negative stative describing that he did not utter a word. The two clauses are joined by و.
segments.s719-g46: Temporal phrase marking duration: هم adds emphasis ('even/indeed'), از ... تا is the standard 'from ... until' construction, نیمه روز = 'mid-day' (noon) and نیم شب = 'mid-night'. Word order is straightforward: 'even from midday until midnight.'
segments.s719-g47: چنین 'thus/so' starts the clause; تا introduces the limit 'until'. شکسته شدش is a periphrastic/passive-like expression: شکسته 'broken' + شدش (شد + enclitic ش = 'befell him' / 'became to him'), so 'until his hands and feet became broken' (i.e. 'until his hands and feet were broken'). The affected body parts دست و پای follow and are the things that 'befell him'.
segments.s719-g48: فکندندش is past tense plural 'they threw / they cast' with the enclitic ـش marking the object 'him'. اندر میان is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the middle (of)'; سرای here means 'house/chamber/room' (a location), so the clause says they threw him into/within the midst of the house.
segments.s719-g137: Imperative/request clause: مَرا (here split as م + را) is the person affected — the speaker as object — and کنید is the 2nd‑person plural/formal imperative of کردن 'to make/do'. دخمه is the direct object 'sepulchre/tomb' and در شهرایران is a prepositional phrase 'in the city of Iran' giving location. Word order is typical Persian V‑final for the verb کنید at the end of the clause.
segments.s719-g138: Second imperative clause coordinated to the first; کنید again is the plural/formal imperative 'make/causes'. بری here functions as an adjective/adverb 'bereft / made free of' modifying the following noun phrase; کاخ بهرام is the palace of Bahram, and ویران is a predicate adjective 'ruined, desolate' that the verb کنید applies to (i.e. 'make Bahram’s palace desolate').
segments.s719-g139: Statement in the past: بسی functions as an adverb 'very/much', رنج is the noun 'suffering', and دیدم is the past form 'I saw/experienced'. ز + خاقان چین is an oblique phrase 'from the Khagan of China' (ز = 'from' in poetic register). Word order is Subject (implicit 'I') + object + verb (past).
segments.s719-g140: Negative experiential clause: ندیدم is 'I did not see' (past negative). که introduces the content clause 'that ...', یک روز 'for one day / even a single day' modifies the clause, کرد is the past-tense 'he did / he made', and آفرین functions as a noun 'acclamation/praise' (so کرد آفرین = 'he uttered praise'). The overall sense is 'I never saw him utter praise even for one day.'
segments.s719-g141: Negated copular clause: نه این بود = 'this was not'. زان رنج پاداش من = 'the reward of that suffering for me' (زان = 'from/that'). Word order: Topic/contrast (نه این) + copula بود (past 'was') + genitive/possessive phrase identifying what was not the case.
segments.s719-g142: Result/clarifying clause introduced by که ('that'): دیو = 'a demon', ی here is an archaic/expletive element (exists/there is) or linking particle, فرستد is the present/subjunctive/optative sense 'should send / would send', and بپرخاش من is the verbal phrase 'to reproach/attack me' (ب‑ prefix + پرخاش). Together it expresses the unwelcome result: 'that a demon would be sent to reproach me.'
segments.s719-g143: Fronting and emphasis: ولیکن همانا = 'but truly / however, verily' (concessive/emphatic). که introduces the subordinate clause; او این سخن = 'he [=that person] this speech' (object/topic). The order is emphatic and poetic but the grammar is standard: discourse particle + subordinate introducer + subject + object.
segments.s719-g144: Conditional protasis and apodosis: اگر بشنود is the conditional 'if he hears' with بشنود as subjunctive/present of شنیدن. سر نداند ز بن is a negative stative clause 'he will not know the head from the root' (نداند = 'he will not know'); ز بن is an ablative/comparative phrase 'from the root/foundation'. The whole line warns that hearing the speech will leave him bewildered.
segments.s719-g105: مرا نیز هم دیو بی‌راه کرد is a compact clause with the object (مرا + را split here) fronted and the verb کرد at the end, a normal Persian SOV order. مرا (here split as م + را) is the first-person object 'me'; نیز/هم are adverbs adding 'also/even' and emphasize that the demon too acted. دیو is the subject and بی‌راه (lit. 'off the path') functions as an object-complement meaning 'led astray; put on the wrong path'; کرد is the past simple verb 'did/caused' that takes the preceding phrase as its object and completes the action.
segments.s719-g106: ز خوبی همان دست کوتاه کرد uses ز + noun to mark source or separation ('from' / 'of'), followed by a noun phrase subject دست (hand/power/help) modified by همان ('that same'). کوتاه کردن here is idiomatic: 'to shorten the hand' = 'to withhold help'; کرد is the past verb 'did/caused' completing the clause. Word order is typical SOV with the verb at the end and modifiers before the noun.
segments.s719-g107: پشیمانم از هرچ کردم ز بد begins with the adjectival-predicative پشیمانم ('I am regretful') where the suffix -م marks first person. از introduces the complement 'about/for' (از هرچ کردم = 'for whatever I did'), هرچ is an indefinite 'whatever', and کردم is the past first-person verb 'I did'. The trailing ز بد clarifies the cause 'because of bad/evil actions.'
segments.s719-g108: کنون‌گر ببخشد ز یزدان سزد is a conditional-adverbial cluster: کنون‌گر (an archaic/poetic compound meaning roughly 'if now' or 'should now') introduces the condition, followed by the subjunctive/ present-future-style ببخشد 'forgives' (third-person). ز یزدان 'by/from God' names the agent/source. سزد is an impersonal present/subjunctive-like verb meaning 'it befits' or 'it is proper', giving a judgment on the condition. The clause order places the conditional adverb before the verb phrase.
segments.s719-g109: نوشته برین گونه بد بر سرم is a nominal phrase where نوشته ('writing' or 'inscription') is followed by a prepositional modifier بر این گونه بد (here orthographically brin/gone) meaning 'of this bad kind/on this manner'. بر سرم 'upon my head' is a standard locative expression; word order places modifiers before the locative complement and there is no finite verb in this verbless descriptive clause (typical in poetry).
segments.s719-g110: غم کرده‌های کهن چون خورم strings together 'غم' (sorrow) with کرده‌های کهن ('old/ancient deeds' or 'long-standing actions') and the verb خورم at the end. خورم is the present/subjunctive form of خوردن in the sense 'to befall' (i.e., 'that befall me'); چون here links as a subordinating/comparative 'as' or 'how', connecting the clause to the notion of the old sorrows. The phrase as a whole describes recurring or ancient sorrows that befall the speaker.
segments.s719-g111: ز تارک کنون آب برتر گذشت begins with ز marking source/causal sense 'from' and تارک کنون (literally 'having left the now' or 'leaving the present') as a participial phrase. آب is the subject 'water' and گذشت is the past 'passed/flowed'. برتر here conveys a sense of 'over/above' or 'higher', so the clause says the water passed over/above after abandoning the present; the verb at the end follows classical SOV order.
segments.s719-g112: غم و شادمانی همه باد گشت is a compact clause where the coordinated subject 'sorrow and joy' is followed by همه ('all/entirely') and the particle باد which here is the optative 'may' or an idiomatic expletive; گشت is the past/passive-like 'became' or 'turned'. Together the phrase reads as a wish/summary: 'may all sorrow and joy become (as nothing)/be blown away', a poetic transformation expressed with the optative flavour of باد + گشت.
segments.s719-g49: به نزدیک + name is a prepositional phrase meaning 'toward/near' someone; classical word order places it before the verb. بازآمدند is the past plural of بازآمدن ('باز-' prefix + آمدن), here 'they came back/returned.' The verb agrees with an understood plural subject (the people).
segments.s719-g50: This is a nominal/adverbial phrase describing the state with which they came: جگر خسته و پرگداز = 'heart(s) weary and burning.' The adjectives follow the noun and coordinate with و; آمدند is repeated (or understood) to link the phrase to the subject: 'they came (with) ...'.
segments.s719-g51: همی‌رفت is an imperfective/past-continuous form meaning 'was going/kept going' or 'kept flowing.' خون is the theme (what was flowing) and از تن خسته is an ablative phrase 'from the weary man's body' (از + تن + adjective). Classical Persian often places the verb early, followed by the noun phrase specifying source.
segments.s719-g52: Structure: [لبان] [پر ز X] = 'lips full of X.' پر (here) is an adjectival/adverbial 'full (of)' taking the preposition ز. The coordinated phrase و رخان لاژورد describes his face/cheeks as lapis-blue (لاژورد = lapis lazuli), so the whole paints the wound-afflicted look: 'lips full of breath/wind and (his) cheeks lapis-blue.'
segments.s719-g53: بیامد is a past tense verb 'came.' هم immediately after it emphasizes 'also/too' (she came as well). اندر زمان = 'at that time/moment.' خواهرش combines خواهر ('sister') with the possessive clitic ش 'his/her' = 'his sister' or in context 'his sister (of the wounded man).'
segments.s719-g54: همه موی is the direct object 'all (the) hair' placed before the verb برکند ('plucked out'). پاک here functions adverbially 'completely/entirely.' از سرش = 'from her head' (از + سر + possessive clitic). The verb form برکند is classical past ('she plucked out').
segments.s719-g55: نهاد here is the past-tense verb 'placed/put' (نهادن). آن سر خسته را is the definite object 'that weary head' (آن + noun + adjective + را object marker). بر کنار = 'to the side/aside' (بر + کنار). Word order: verb + object + locative complement is normal in classical lines.
segments.s719-g56: همی‌کرد is an imperfective/past-continuous 'was doing' (ongoing past action). با خویشتن literally 'with herself' (i.e., to/against herself). کار زار = 'a wretched/lamentable act' where کار = 'act/affair' and زار qualifies it as pitiable; together the phrase says she was committing a lamentable deed upon herself.
segments.s719-g121: This is a vocative + indirect object phrase. زبدها (vocative plural) addresses the elite/nobles; جهاندارتان is جهاندار (sovereign) with the 2nd‑person plural possessive suffix -تان (“your sovereign”); یاربس is a proper name (the addressee or the sovereign's name). Word order places the vocative first, then the possessed noun phrase.
segments.s719-g122: مگویید is a prohibitive/present form addressing plural addressees (‘do not say’ / ‘do not tell’). زاندوه is the prepositional/compound form ز + اندوه = ‘of/from sorrow’; و شادی joins ‘and joy’; بکس means ‘to/for any one person’ or ‘a single person’. Together the phrase gives the content and the indirect object of the prohibition: ‘do not tell (to) any one person the matters of sorrow and joy.’
segments.s719-g123: نبودم is the first‑person past negative of بودن (‘I was not’ / ‘there was not for me’). بگیتی is the locative/poetic b- prefix + گیتی ‘in the world’. جزین (جز + این) means ‘except this’. نیز بهر is an emphatic connector meaning ‘besides/also for’ or ‘other than this’. Grammatically this clause states that, in the world, there was nothing for the speaker except the thing just mentioned.
segments.s719-g124: سرآمد here means ‘the conclusion/end’ (the coming of the end). کنون = ‘now’. رفتنی‌ام is a predicative participial/adjectival form with the 1st‑person copula ‘‑ام’, literally ‘I am going/ready to go’ — ‘I am about to depart’. ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’ and دهر = ‘the world/age’. The sequence states that the speaker’s end has arrived and he must leave the world.
segments.s719-g125: یلان is the plural noun ‘heroes’ (subject). سینه here means ‘breast/heart’ (metonymically ‘from their hearts’ or ‘sincerely’). راگفت is را + گفت (‘they said’/‘said’ with the object marker fused in older usage); یکسر modifies the speech as ‘at once’/‘altogether’. سپاه is the direct object ‘the army’ (or what is being spoken about). Together: ‘The heroes, from their hearts, said at once…’.
segments.s719-g126: سپردم is the 1st‑person past transitive ‘I entrusted/handed over’. تو is the 2nd‑person singular object ‘you’ (or vocative ‘O you’ depending on context). رابخت here is a fused construction with the object marker را attached (ra+بخت); given the lexical list this token is best analyzed as the object marker construction. بیدارخواه appears as a proper/name‑like compound (literal 'seeker of wakefulness') and functions as a vocative or epithet. The clause reports the content of the heroes’ speech: ‘I entrusted the army to you, O Bidārkhwāh.’
segments.s719-g127: نگه کن is an imperative: نگه (look/watch/care) + کن (do) = ‘take care’ / ‘look after’. The following phrase should be read together as بدین خواهرپاک تن = ‘this chaste sister’s body’ (بدین = ‘this/with this’, خواهرپاک = ‘chaste sister’, تن = ‘body’). Word order is imperative + prepositional/object phrase: ‘Take care of this chaste sister’s body.’ Note: the tokenization separates ب and دین and lists دین with senses that do not match this demonstrative use; this demonstrative reading is idiomatic in the classical text.
segments.s719-g128: زگیتی is the poetic compound z‑ + گیتی ‘from/of the world’ or ‘worldly’. بس is an interjection/adverb ‘enough/stop’ or ‘no more’. اومرتو and رارای are uncertain/opaque readings in the manuscript tradition (they appear to be fused or corrupt forms); grammatically the sequence appears to give an instruction or verdict about the woman (زن = ‘woman’). Given the context, the clause functions as a follow‑up imperative: ‘Enough of worldly matters — carry out your command/regard concerning the woman.’ Because several tokens are manuscript‑uncertain, their precise senses are marked as unknown.
segments.s719-g113: نوشته (here) is a past-participial/nominal use meaning 'written' (i.e. 'it was written' or 'the writing'); چنين is a demonstrative/adverb 'thus'; بود is the past copula 'was'; وبود is a poetic conjunctional repetition 'and was' (literally 'and it was'); آنچ = 'that which' (relative/demonstrative); بود (final) repeats the past copula. The clause literally states that the writing/read account was thus — 'it was written thus, and that which was, was.'
segments.s719-g114: This clause is a general statement about the written record. نوشته here functions as a noun 'the writing / what is written'; نکاهد is the negative present/optative form meaning 'does not lessen' (classical usage where ن- negates the verb); نه هرگز 'nor ever / not at all, ever'; فزود is the verb 'increased' (here in sense 'to increase'), giving the idea 'the written neither diminishes nor ever increases.'
segments.s719-g115: همان 'that very' (demonstrative) modifies پند 'advice'. تویادگارمنست is a fused classical sequence: تو (possessive 'your' attached postpositively to the noun) + یادگارِ من است 'is my memento/keepsake'. Word order in older Persian allows the possessor pronoun after the noun, and the predicate 'yâdgâr-e man ast' is fused here.
segments.s719-g116: سخنهای is the plural/construct form 'the words / sayings (of yours)'; توگوشوارمنست is another fused sequence meaning '(they) are pleasing to the ear for me' — literally 'your (saying) is an ornament/pleasure to my ear / you are ear-ornament to me.' The predicate ends in -منست = 'is mine' (classical 'man ast').
segments.s719-g117: سرآمد here = 'the end/conclusion' (noun) and کنون = 'now'; کار 'affair/matter'; بیداد 'injustice/oppression' and داد 'justice' form a paired contrast. The phrase reports that the matter concerning injustice and justice has come to an end (literal word order: 'end now the affair of injustice and justice').
segments.s719-g118: سخنهات 'your words/speeches' (object) + برمن 'toward/on me' (prepositional phrase). مکن is the negative imperative 'do not' (second person); نیزیاد is a classical compound meaning 'no more' / 'any longer' (نی- negative prefix + زیاد 'more'). Together: 'Do not [address] your speeches to me any more.'
segments.s719-g119: شماروی is a compound/nominal meaning 'the reckoning/count'; راسوی 'facing/toward' (here 'before' or 'in the presence of'); یزدان = 'God'; کنید is the plural/formal imperative 'do (make)'. The line commands: 'place/account your reckoning before God' (imperative plural).
segments.s719-g120: همه 'all/everyone'; پشت بربخت is an older phrase 'on/against fate' or 'toward fortune' (پشت + بر بخت), here meaning roughly 'with respect to fate/fortune'; خندان is an adjective 'smiling/cheerful'; کنید (again) is the imperative 'make' (plural). Together the clause is an idiomatic wish/command: 'make everyone's fortune smile' — i.e. bring happiness/good luck to all.
segments.s719-g17: بشد is an archaic narrative verb meaning 'it happened / he began / he ran' used here to introduce the action. پرده‌دار گرامی is the subject noun phrase 'the honored chamberlain' (پرده‌دار = chamberlain/curtain‑keeper; گرامی = honored). دوان is a present‑participle/verb meaning 'running' placed after the subject to form the predication 'ran off'.
segments.s719-g18: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so' linking to the preceding narration. تا here marks extent/goal ('as far as / to'), so تا در خانه پهلوان = 'to/into the champion's house.' در is the preposition/noun 'door/inside' used as location, خانه 'house', پهلوان 'champion/hero'.
segments.s719-g19: چنین گفت is a formulaic narrative 'thus it was said / thus he said'. کامد is an archaic verbal form 'came / arrived' (used here as a simple past narrative). یکی بدنشان = 'one of their people' (یکی = a certain one / someone; بدنشان = 'their person(s)', i.e. one of them).
segments.s719-g20: فرستاده is the noun 'messenger' (here appositive to 'one of their men'). و = 'and'. پوستینی کشان is a participial phrase: پوستینی 'a fur coat' and کشان 'dragging' (present participle), so 'a messenger, dragging/with a trailing fur cloak.'
segments.s719-g21: همی‌گوید is a present/habitual narrative verb often used where English uses past ('he said' / 'he kept saying'). از marks source 'from'; دخت خاقان = 'the khagan's daughter' (دخت = daughter/maiden; خاقان = khagan); پیام = 'message'. Together: 'he says/said a message from the khagan's daughter.'
segments.s719-g22: رسانم here functions as an instruction/intent 'deliver / convey' (the verb رساندن with the enclitic form used in imperatival/reporting contexts). The text splits بدین into ب + دین: ب = 'to/into' (prepositional prefix) and دین here intends the demonstrative 'this/that' (split and not listed in the provided senses). مهتر شادکام is the indirect object/appositive 'the fortunate chief' (مهتر = chief/headman; شادکام = fortunate). The whole phrase = 'deliver this to the fortunate chief.'
segments.s719-g23: چنین گفت is the narrative formula 'thus he said.' بهرام کو را (written as کو را) here functions as a proper name phrase 'Bahrām Kura' (کورا is a proper name/place epithet). بگوی is an imperative/infinitival form 'tell' (the verbal root 'گفتن' in an imperative/command sense).
segments.s719-g24: که introduces the subordinate clause of the command ('that / so that'). هم = 'also/even' emphasizing inclusion. زان = 'from that' (that + ablative), در here means 'door/gate' (not just 'in'), خانه = 'house', بنمای is an imperative 'show! / reveal!' (archaic benmåy), and روی = 'face' (object). Together: 'that he also show his face from that door of the house.'
segments.s719-g81: برین is the fused prepositional phrase بر + این ('for/with these' or 'about these'); کرده‌ها is the plural noun 'deeds'. The remainder بر پشیمان بری is a short predicate: پشیمان ('regretful/remorse') with بری functioning as an adjective/participial predicate 'free from / spared (of)'. The whole segment expresses a wish/optative sense: 'May you be spared remorse for these deeds.'
segments.s719-g82: گنهکار جان is a nominative/oblique phrase 'the sinner’s life/soul' (جان = life/soul). پیش یزدان is the prepositional phrase 'in the presence of / before God'. Again بری is used as 'spared / free from' so the clause reads as a prayer: 'May the sinner’s life be spared before God.'
segments.s719-g83: بد آمد is an impersonal/past construction 'it came ill / it fared badly'. بدین here functions as a dative/adverbial 'to this' (classical 'به + این' → 'to/for this'), followed by خاندان بزرگ 'the great family/lineage' as the affected party: 'It fared ill for this great house.'
segments.s719-g84: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject; میش 'ewe' is a simile/metaphor and گشتیم is the first‑person plural past of گشتن 'to become/turn into' → 'we became (e.g. like) ewes'. و links the second clause: دشمن 'the enemy' + چو 'like/as' + گرک 'wolf' → 'and the enemy like a wolf.' Word order is Subject – predicative noun – verb, then conjunction + comparative clause.
segments.s719-g85: چو آن خسته بشنید is a temporal clause: چو = 'when', آن خسته = 'that weary one', بشنید is past 'heard'. گفتار او 'his speech/words' is the object of بشنید. Word order is standard classical Persian: temporal clause → subject → verb → object.
segments.s719-g86: بدید is the past tense 'saw/perceived'. آن here functions as a demonstrative 'that' modifying the coordinated phrase دل و رای هشیار او = 'his heart and his alert counsel/opinion' (دل = heart, رای = mind/counsel, هشیار = 'alert/sober'). The verb precedes the coordinated noun phrase for emphasis: 'that heart and his discerning counsel saw it.'
segments.s719-g87: به is the preposition 'with/by/at'; ناخن رخان is a descriptive phrase 'nails loosened/relaxed' (رخان = 'loose/relaxed'); خسته 'weary' qualifies the state; و links to کنده موی 'with hair torn out' (کنده = 'torn out', موی = 'hair'). The whole phrase is a circumstantial adjunct describing appearance/condition.
segments.s719-g88: پر از خون constructs 'full of blood' (پر = 'full (of)'), modifying دل 'heart' (heart full of blood). و coordinates دیده 'eye(s)' and the following پر آب 'full of water' (آب used figuratively = tears), while روی 'face' indicates location. Word order stacks the two images: heart full of blood, and eyes full of water on the face.
segments.s719-g57: همی‌گفت is a literary/habitual-past expression (literally 'kept saying') placing the verb before the rest for poetic effect. زار‌ای here functions as a lamenting exclamation/adverb ('lamentingly' or 'alas') addressing or characterizing the utterance. سوار دلیر is a noun plus postpositive adjective ('the brave rider'); Persian often places the adjective after the noun.
segments.s719-g58: کزو is a fused prepositional adverb meaning 'from which / from whom'. بیشه is the place noun 'thicket/grove' and is the source referenced by کزو. بگذاشتی is 2nd-person singular past of گذاشتن (here 'you left/put [behind]'); the verb refers to the addressee. نره شیر is a noun phrase with نره ('male' for an animal) modifying شیر ('lion'), literally 'male lion'.
segments.s719-g59: که functions as a relative/conjunction linking this clause to what precedes ('for/that'). برد is the past form of بردن here meaning 'removed/took away' (classical use). این ستون جهان را is the definite object marked by را ('this pillar of the world' as direct object). ز جا is a prepositional phrase 'from its place' (ز = from, جا = place).
segments.s719-g60: براندیشه here is an exhortative/imperative-like phrase ('consider / think upon / hold in thought'), followed by بد ('ill/bad') as the object ('think ill of him'). که introduces the following explanatory clause; بد رهنما is a noun phrase 'a bad guide' giving the reason or characterization. The repetition of بد emphasizes his badness.
segments.s719-g61: الا‌ای is a vocative/exclamatory formula ('behold, O ...'). سوار سپهبد is an apposition sequence meaning 'mounted spahbed/general' (سوار 'rider/horseman' with سپهبد 'spahbed/general'). تنا appears immediately after as an epithet or name element; although its dictionary senses list 'body/torso', here it functions as a proper-name/epithet in apposition to the titles.
segments.s719-g62: This segment strings epithets/titles with و as the simple coordinating conjunction ('and'). جهانگیر is used as a personal title/name ('Jahangir' / 'world-conqueror'), ناباک is an epithet meaning 'virtuous/pure' modifying the person, and شیر اوژنا is another epithet 'the lion Owjna' where اوژنا is an archaic element (here functioning as a name or intensifying epithet rather than its literal lexical senses).
segments.s719-g63: نه ... و نه is the correlative negative construction 'neither ... nor'. خسرو here functions generically as 'king' within the compound خسروپرست ('king-worshipper'); پرست is the '-worshipper' suffix forming a compound noun. ایزدپرست is an analogous compound 'god-worshipper'.
segments.s719-g64: تن پیل‌وار سپهبد is a descriptive noun phrase: تن 'body' modified by پیل‌وار 'elephant-like' producing 'with an elephant-like body', followed by سپهبد 'spahbed/general' in apposition. که is the relative pronoun 'who/which' introducing the relative clause, and خست is an adjectival/predicative past form meaning 'was/is weary/tired' (an older form used in epic diction).
segments.s719-g97: کجا شد is an interrogative + past copular/verb (کجا = 'where'; شد = 'became/did it happen') asking 'where did it happen/become'. به گفتار دیوان is a prepositional phrase using به meaning 'according to/by' (here marking the source of the report) with گفتار 'account/speech' and دیوان 'the divan/record/court' as its possessor; ز شاه is a poetic genitive 'of the king' introduced by ز ('from/of'). The whole segment frames the question as 'where, according to the divan's account of the king...'.
segments.s719-g98: جهان کرد is verb + object where جهان ('the world') is the thing acted on and کرد ('made/did') is the simple past/preterite 'made/caused'. بر خویشتن uses the preposition بر 'upon' plus the emphatic reflexive خویشتن 'himself' (i.e. 'upon himself'), and بر سیاه is a prepositional phrase 'into/onto blackness' (سیاه = 'dark/gloomy'). Grammatically this says the subject caused the world to become dark with respect to himself: 'made the world black upon himself.'
segments.s719-g99: همان نیز بیدار is an anaphoric phrase: همان 'that very one' + نیز 'also' + بیدار 'awake/alert' (predicate/adjective). کاوس is the proper name Kay Kāvus; کی here appears after the name in inverted poetic order and functions like the interrogative/title element 'کی' (in epic names normally 'کی' indicates the royal title 'Kay'); the phrase presents the same person as Kay Kāvus who is awake/alert. Word order is poetic/inverted but the elements are appositive epithets.
segments.s719-g100: جهاندار is an epithet meaning 'sovereign/world-ruler' used as a title; نیک اختر is a compound adjective 'of good/auspicious star' (نیک modifying اختر), and نیک‌پی is a compound adjective meaning 'well-born' or 'of good parentage'. Together they are appositive epithets qualifying Kay Kāvus. Adjectives follow or are placed next to the noun/epithet in this poetic listing.
segments.s719-g101: تبه شد is a verbal phrase meaning 'it went wrong/it became bad' (تبه = bad/ruinous; شد = became). به گفتار here again means 'according to the speech/account of', and دیو پلید is 'the vile demon' (دیو = demon, پلید = vile) — the last adjective modifies دیو. The clause reports that, per the demon's telling, things turned out badly.
segments.s719-g102: شنیدی is the past second-person/suppletive perfect 'you heard/you have heard' (here functioning like 'you know/you have heard of'); بدیها is the plural object 'the evils/misfortunes'. که introduces a relative/content clause 'that', او را is the clitic pronoun 'him' with the definite/direct-object marker را, and رسید 'reached/arrived' means 'befell' (i.e. 'that befell him'). The clause says 'you have heard the bad things that befell him.'
segments.s719-g103: همان 'that same' is the subject/anaphor; به آسمان شد is a motion/result phrase 'went up/ascended into the sky' (به + آسمان with شد 'became/went' — here 'went up to the sky'). که introduces a subordinate clause 'so that/that' and گردان سپهر is a poetic noun phrase 'the revolving/turning heavens' (گردان = revolving, سپهر = the heavens).
segments.s719-g104: ببیند is a present/subjunctive or narrative-present 'may see/does see' (here narrative: 'that the heavens saw'); پراگندن is a verbal noun 'the scattering/dispersion', and ماه و مهر are the coordinated celestial bodies 'moon and (sun/deity)' — مهر here functions as a celestial/solar referent in poetry. segments.s719-g9: This clause is personal pronoun + agent phrase + perfect verb. من is the 1st-person subject 'I'. از دخت خاقان is an agent/source phrase 'by the khaghan's daughter' (از marks the sender/agent here). فرستاده‌ام is the perfect (past) form with the 1st-person enclitic -ام; in this context it functions as a passive/perfect meaning 'I have been sent' or 'I was sent.'
segments.s719-g10: This is a correlative negation built with نه ... نه ('neither ... nor'). جنگی کسی‌ام reads as a nominal predicate 'I am not a warrior/fighter' (جنگی used as 'warlike/fighter') with کسی‌ام 'I am a person (of that sort)'. آزاده‌ام is a second nominal predicate 'I am not a noble/free‑born' — the -ام ending marks 1st person present/perfect copula. The two predicates are linked by the repeated نه to give 'neither ... nor.'
segments.s719-g11: Poetic word order: object (یکی راز) precedes the verb گفت, followed by the subject آن زن پارسا. یکی راز = 'a certain secret' (یکی = a certain/one). گفت is the past tense 'said/told' (here used as 'told'), and آن زن پارسا is the subject 'that pious woman.' The O–V–S order is a stylistic inversion common in poetry.
segments.s719-g12: بدان is the imperative/hortative formed from ب + دان meaning 'know/learn this' (b- prefixed to دان). تا introduces a purpose/result clause 'so that' and is followed by the subjunctive/present tense بگویم 'that I may say/tell.' بدین پادشا is a prepositional phrase (ب- + این = 'to this') plus پادشا 'king', meaning 'to this king' — together: 'learn it so that I may tell it to this king.'
segments.s719-g13: ز here functions poetically as 'because of / out of'. مهر ورا = 'love for her' (مهر 'love' followed by the older object form ورا = 'her'). از در بستن is an idiomatic verbal phrase 'to shut the door on / to bar someone'; بستن is the infinitive/masdar and است is the copula in present tense, so the clause says in effect 'it is (that) the door is shut on her out of love' — the older word order places the prepositional element before the verb.
segments.s719-g14: همان 'that/same' refers back to the woman just mentioned; نیز means 'also'. بیمار و آبستن is a coordination 'sick and pregnant' with the copula است 'is' concluding the predicate. Word order is straightforward: demonstrative + adverb + adjectives + copula.
segments.s719-g15: گر is the conditional 'if'. آگه کنی is a subjunctive/imperative-type clause 'if you make (me) aware / if you tell (me)' — the object (me) is omitted and understood. تا introduces the purpose/result clause that follows; رسانم is 1st-person (subjunctive/optative) 'that I may deliver' and پیام is its object. The sequence places the verb رسانم before its object پیام (poetic inversion) but the meaning is 'so that I may deliver the message'.
segments.s719-g16: بدین here is the prepositional phrase 'به + این' = 'to this' (poetic spelling/compaction). It governs تاجور 'merchant/trader', modified by مهتر 'chief/elder' and the epithet نیک‌نام 'of good repute'. segments.s719-g25: بیامد is a past tense verb 'came' placed before the subject (a common poetic word order). قلون here is the name/agent; تا ... marks the limit 'as far as / up to'. به نزدیک در is a prepositional phrase 'to/near the door' (به = toward/into, نزدیک = near, در = door/gate rather than the locative 'in').
segments.s719-g26: This clause continues the action: بکاف is used as a verb 'struck (with the palm)' and is followed by the place 'در' (door). در خانه here reads 'the door of the house / the doorway' and بنهاد is an archaic past-form meaning 'he put/placed' (بنهاد سر = 'placed his head'); سر is the direct object 'head'. The sequence is V + object phrases, typical of Ferdowsi's elevated style.
segments.s719-g27: چو is a conjunction 'when'. دیدش is past 'saw' with the object clitic -ش = 'him/it' (so 'he saw him/it'). یکی پیر بد سست و زار is a noun with a string of adjectives following it ('a certain/one old man, ill, weak, and wretched') — adjective order after the noun is normal in older Persian.
segments.s719-g28: بدو is an indirect-object clitic phrase 'to him / unto him'. گفت is the past 'said' introducing reported speech (the provided lemma senses are noun-like and do not include the verbal past, so the correct verbal sense is indicated below). گرنامه is a literary word for 'letter/message'. داری is present 'you have' (a possession verb) so the clause reads as a conditional request, and بیار is the imperative 'bring (it)'.
segments.s719-g29: قلون گفت = 'Qalon said' (again گفت used as past verb). شاها is the vocative 'O king!'. پیامست is a nominal sentence 'is a message' (پیام + است fused as پیامست). و بس is a final particle 'and that's enough / that's all' giving closure to the statement.
segments.s719-g30: نخواهم is the negative of خواهم ('I will not / I do not wish'); it governs the subordinate clause with که + present-subjunctive. گویم here is the present-subjunctive 'that I say/tell' (the provided lemma senses were not the verbal reading, so marked separately). سخن پیش کس = 'the matter/speech in front of anyone' — پیش کس = 'before anyone / in anyone's presence'; together the clause means 'I will not tell the matter before anyone.'
segments.s719-g31: ورا گفت = 'he said to him' (ورا is dative 'to him'). زود اندر آی is an imperative phrase 'come in quickly / come quickly inside' (اندر = 'in/into', آی = imperative 'come'). و بگوی continues the command with an aorist/imperative-like form 'and speak / and tell' (here the verbal sense of بگوی is imperative/subjunctive).
segments.s719-g32: This is the content of the command: بگوشم literally 'to my ear' (dative), functioning as the indirect object of the verb 'speak' (implied). نهانی is an adverb 'secretly'. بهانه is the noun 'excuse / pretext' and مجوی is a negative imperative 'do not seek' (مجوی governs بهانه: 'seek no pretext'). The word order places the adverb before the object and the negated imperative last, yielding 'whisper it into my ear secretly; look for no excuse.'
segments.s719-g129: مباشید is a negative imperative/optative addressed to a plural 'you' (literally 'do not be' / 'may you not be'). یک تن 'one person' is the noun phrase being negated; زدیگر (زِ دیگر) here means 'from one another' or 'separate from the others' and جدا is an adverb/adjective 'apart'. segments.s719-g130: جدایی 'separation' is the noun; مبادا is a conventional negative jussive ('may it not be' / 'let it not happen') that scopes over جدایی; میان شما is the prepositional phrase 'among you'. Together this yields 'may separation not be among you.'
segments.s719-g131: برین is the poetic spelling of بر این ('on/at this'); بوم 'homeland/soil' combined with دشمن gives 'enemy/hostile land'. ممانید is a negative imperative/plural of ماندن 'do not stay/remain'. دیر here is an adverb 'for a long time' (i.e. 'do not remain long').
segments.s719-g132: که introduces a causal/explanatory clause ('for/because'); رفتیم and وگشتیم are perfective past plural verbs 'we went' and 'we wandered/returned' presented as joint actions. ازگاه is an adverbial 'from/since (the time/place of)'; سیر here functions as a noun 'journey/travelling' (poetic/archaic use), so the clause explains they have been travelling/roaming.
segments.s719-g133: همه 'everyone' is the addressee; یکسره here is an adverb meaning 'straightaway/at once'; پیش 'before/in the presence of' and خسرو 'Khosrow' form the goal phrase. شوید is the optative/imperative plural of شدن ('become' / here used idiomatically as 'go/come into the presence of'), so the clause means 'all go at once before Khosrow.'
segments.s719-g134: بگویید is an imperative plural 'say/tell' (addressing the group); و 'and' links the two imperatives; گفتار او is 'his speech/words' (object); بشنوید is imperative plural 'hear/listen'. Together: 'speak (to him) and listen to what he says.'
segments.s719-g135: گر is the conditional particle 'if'; آمرزش 'pardon/forgiveness' is the subject of آید 'comes' (present/subjunctive/habitual form used in classical style). شما is the addressed 'you' (here the potential recipient of pardon). راز شاه 'the king's secret' is placed after the pronoun by poetic inversion and is the content referred to in the next clause.
segments.s719-g136: جز او 'except him' excludes everyone but that person; رامخوانید is a poetic imperative plural (literally 'call/read/tame aloud') here meaning 'call out' / 'address' or more idiomatically 'reveal/tell' to (so: 'tell no one but him'); خورشید و ماه 'the sun and the moon' are added as witnesses invoked in the idiom. segments.s719-g145: نبود is a past existential/negation-like verb here ('there was not' / 'it was nothing'), followed by این 'this' (referring to the foregoing) and جز 'except', so the phrase literally says 'this was nothing except from the deeds of the Iranians.' segments.s719-g146: همی here functions as an emphatic/limiting particle ('indeed/just'), followed by دیو بد رهنمون 'an evil demon (دیو بد) [as] guide (رهنمون)'; درمیـان is a locative adverb 'in their midst/among (them)'. segments.s719-g147: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past verb 'he commanded/said'; پس is an adverb 'then'; تا introduces a subordinate/jussive clause ('so that/that'), and بیامد here is jussive/subjunctive 'come' (not simple past) — together: 'he ordered that the scribe come.' دبیر is the addressee/agent 'the scribe'.
segments.s719-g148: نویسد is a jussive/present-form of 'to write' used as the action required by the command ('to write'); یکی نامه‌ای is the direct object 'a letter' (یکی = one/a), and بر حریر specifies the medium 'on/of silk' (بر + noun = 'on silk').
segments.s719-g149: بگوید functions as a reportive/imperative-type verb introducing indirect speech ('say/tell'), followed by the indirect object (بخاقان 'to the khaghan') and a content clause introduced by که 'that' which contains the report about بهرام رفت. segments.s719-g151: تو is the vocative/subject 'you'; این ماندگان is a genitival/nominal phrase 'those who remain / the remaining ones'; راز من is the object 'my secret'. یاددار here should be read as an imperative-like commissive 'keep/remember' (ِیاد دار), so the whole clause means 'you, keep/remember my secret for those who remain.' segments.s719-g152: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; رنج و بد دشمن lists threats 'suffering and the evil enemy'; آزاد دار is an imperative verbal phrase 'keep free' (آزاد = free, دار = keep/hold), so the clause is an order 'keep them free from suffering and the wicked enemy.' segments.s719-g161: همی is an archaic/progressive particle that gives a continuous or ongoing aspect to the verb that follows. خون خروشید is a compressed, poetic expression: خون (literally “blood”) + خروشید (“roared/cryed out”), here used metaphorically to describe a violent, anguished cry; خواهر is the grammatical subject placed after the verb (typical V–S order in poetry); ز درد is a causal prepositional phrase (‘from/because of pain’) explaining the reason for the crying.
segments.s719-g162: سخنهای او is a possessive noun phrase (‘his words/utterances’) with او as possessor; یک به یک is an adverbial idiom meaning ‘one by one’ (here placed before the verb); یاد کرد is a compound where the noun یاد (‘remembrance’) combines with the light verb کرد (‘did/made’) to mean ‘remembered’ — یاد is the lexical core and کرد is the verbal auxiliary.
segments.s719-g163: ز تیمار او marks cause/ground (‘because of his/her care’); شد is the past of شدن used as a copular verb meaning ‘became’; دلش (‘her heart’) is the possessed noun with the clitic ش; به دونیم is a result phrase meaning ‘into two (halves)’ — together دلش به دونیم شد = ‘her heart was split in two.’
segments.s719-g164: یکی functions as an indefinite numeral here (‘a/one’); تنگ تابوت is a noun phrase ‘a narrow coffin’ with تنگ as adjective before the noun تابوت; کردش is the verb کرد with the clitic ش indicating the person for whom the action was done (‘she made for him’); ز سیم is a material/genitive phrase ‘of silver’ specifying the coffin’s material.

segments.s719-g166: قصب here is the direct object meaning ‘a reed/cane’; کرد is the transitive verb ‘did/made/placed’ used with that object; the location is given by the prepositional phrase در زیر پیراهنش (‘in/under his shirt’) where در + زیر combine to mark position and ش is the possessive clitic on پیراهن.
segments.s719-g167: همی‌ریخت is progressive/past continuous (‘was pouring/was scattering’); کافور is the material being scattered; گرد is an adverb/postposition meaning ‘around/about’; اندرش is اندر + possessive clitic (in/within him) so the phrase means ‘around him’ — the clause describes ongoing action of scattering camphor about him.
segments.s719-g168: بدین گونه is a fixed adverbial phrase ‘in this way/in this manner’ ( here written in two parts in the word list: ب as prefix and دین+گونه forming the phrase); برتا means ‘up to/until’ or ‘as far as’; نهان شد is a verbal phrase (‘became hidden/concealed’) with شد as auxiliary; سرش (‘his head’) is the possessed noun — together: ‘in this way his head became hidden.’
